title: Release-free electro-optomechanical crystal modulator
authors: Paul Burger, Joey Frey, Johan Kolvik, Mads B. Kristensen, Raphaël van Laer
abstract: Electro-optic modulation is central to classical optical communications and emerging quantum technologies. High-confinement optomechanical crystal modulators enable microwave-optical transduction through strong optomechanical interactions and offer a promising interface between superconducting qubits and optical fibers. However, their performance is limited by thermal noise from optical absorption. Release-free optomechanical crystals provide improved thermal anchoring but have not yet been integrated into a microwave-optical transducer. Here, we demonstrate a release-free electro-optomechanical transducer combining strong optomechanical interactions in silicon with the efficient piezoelectricity of lithium niobate via micro-transfer printing. We observe electro- and optomechanical coupling rates compatible with quantum-level operation when co-integrated with a superconducting microwave circuit. This advance moves release-free electro-optomechanical devices toward practical microwave-optical interfaces.
summary: The researchers developed a new type of 'release-free' optomechanical crystal that stays attached to its substrate to improve heat dissipation. By integrating lithium niobate with silicon, they created a device capable of converting microwave signals to optical signals. This is a significant step toward building practical interfaces for connecting quantum computers to optical networks.
main_problem: Traditional suspended optomechanical crystals suffer from thermal noise due to optical absorption and lack efficient thermal anchoring, which limits their use as transducers between superconducting qubits and optical fibers.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a functional release-free electro-optomechanical transducer that achieves efficient microwave-to-optical transduction with coupling rates compatible with quantum-level operation.
method: The device was fabricated by integrating thin-film lithium niobate onto a silicon-on-insulator platform via micro-transfer printing and characterized using tunable lasers, vector network analyzers, and high-speed photodetectors.
model_or_system: A release-free, chip-scale electro-optomechanical crystal transducer on a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) platform, combining silicon for optomechanics and lithium niobate for piezoelectricity.
key_observables: Optical resonance, mechanical linewidth, optomechanical coupling rate (g_om), electromechanical efficiency, and microwave-to-optical scattering parameter (S_oe).
important_parameters: Resolved-sideband regime (omega_m > kappa_o), low cooperativity regime (C_om << 1), mechanical frequency of ~4.32 GHz, and total microwave-to-optical efficiency of 1.5e-7.
main_assumptions: The transduction is assumed to be of piezo-optomechanical origin rather than purely electro-optic; simulations assume an isotropic loss factor to account for intrinsic material loss.
figures_summary: Fig 2 shows the mechanical spectrum and phonon number vs microwave power; Fig 4a compares measured and simulated microwave-to-optical scattering parameters; Fig 4b visualizes mechanical mode profiles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of thermal noise in suspended crystals, describes the fabrication of the release-free LN-on-SOI platform, details the experimental characterization of optical and mechanical properties, presents the results of microwave-to-optical transduction, and discusses the potential for scaling to qubit-level operation.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a hardware pathway for efficient, thermally-anchored interfaces between microwave-frequency superconducting qubits and optical-frequency communication fibers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Release-free electro-optomechanical transducer in silicon-on-insulator. a) Principle of the transduction process. An optical waveg- uide is evanescently coupled to an optical mode ˆa which optomechanically interacts with mechanical mode ˆb. The latter is connected to a mi- crowave line through piezoelectric conversion. b) Material stacks in the optomechanical and electromechanical regions. c) Normalized transver- sal optical field, Ey, and mechanical displacement field, u, of the simulated transducer design. d) Scanning electron microscope image of a comparable device (scale bar: 10 µm). The inset shows a false-colored zoom-in of the electro-optomechanical region (scale bar: 1 µm).

Fig. 2. Room-temperature electro-optic transduction. a) Mechanical spectrum while a constant frequency microwave tone is applied to the piezoelectric section. The colors correspond to varying microwave power as shown in b). b) Phonon numbers extracted from a) as a function of microwave power. The number of coherently generated phonons rises linearly with power while the thermal population is unaffected. c) Microwave to optical scattering parameter (uncalibrated) as a function of frequency (blue) and simulated response (purple; described in main text and appendix C). The simulated spectrum was shifted by −160 MHz to align the two. The thermal mechanical spectrum (inset; red; background...

Fig. 3. Classical electro-optomechanical transmission of bit ar- ray. a) Simplified schematic of the measurement scheme used in place of the VNA employed for the microwave-to-optical charac- terization in Sec. 3 (appendix D). For acousto-optic modulation an arbitrary digital signal with amplitude V0 = 100 mV is up- and down- converted to/from the transduction band. b) The power spectrum of an upconverted square pulse train with fundamental frequency 0.5 MHz on top of the thermal optomechanical signal. c) Eye dia- grams of a non-return-to-zero bit array measured in time domain after demodulation for two different bit rates R. The red lines show fits to exponential ring-up and ring down.

Fig. 4. Microwave-to-optics transduction. a) Measured (blue) and simulated (purple) microwave-to-optical scattering parameter. The measured data was shifted by +160 MHz to align the spectra. The measured Soe is uncalibrated. The simulated Soe and its origin is described in the text. b) Mechanical mode profiles corresponding to selected driving frequencies in a). Colorbar unit: pm.

Figure 4 shows the measured and simulated scattering parameters along with the mode profiles. The measured data was shifted by +160 MHz to align the general characteristics of the two spectra, such as peaks, dips and cutoff frequency. We find reasonable qualitative agreement indicating that even the transduction bands measured away from the main peaks are of piezo-optomechanical origin, and not e.g. electro-optic. The sharp cut-off at 4.5 GHz coincides with an emerging quasi-bandgap at that frequency in the partial mirror region of that device.
